metadata.title: Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car-following data

abstract: Situational discomfort awareness plays an important role in decision making among drivers and has rarely been discussed in detail in previous research. An instrumented vehicle was used to collect car-following data from multiple drivers, thereby quantitatively examining situational discomfort grading patterns using a new discomfort grading method and the latent Dirichlet allocation model. In this process, the gas pedal data and speed difference data are particularly involved in the computation for providing broader meaning to discomfort and building more comprehensive situations. The results show that individual discomfort awareness varies between drivers. More importantly, the potential patterns of situational discomfort grading are extracted, which provides knowledge for characterizing drivers in the context of discomfort awareness. The knowledge achieved can be further applied to distinguish drivers and identify the typical comfort and discomfort zones. This study has great value for promoting investigations on traffic psychology and developing more effective and customized driver assistant systems.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: During the driving process, drivers always tend to put themselves in situations that make them feel comfortable, and they take actions according to their discomfort level. Due to characteristics, such as age, driving experience, temper and habit, drivers gradually shape their own patterns for identifying and grading the comfort or discomfort level of encountered situations. Quantitatively mining and distinguishing such grading patterns of drivers can help theoretically improve the issues in traffic psychology, such as understanding decision making among drivers and modelling driving behaviour. Meanwhile, this type of analysis can also benefit Advanced Driver Assistant Systems (ADAS), i.e., Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems, as well as autonomous driving systems by reducing the occurrence of discomfort with customized behaviour mechanisms, thereby increasing the use of these systems (Mohtavipour et al., 2017;Pereira et al., 2015;Siebert et al., 2017).

Gibson and Crooks were the first to propose the margins that are kept between drivers and other road users for safety and comfort (Gibson and Crooks, 1938). Taking the margin as an archetype, Näätänen and Summala further proposed the concept of the comfort zone (Näätänen and Summala, 1974). When drivers cross the comfort zone, they feel uncomfortable and attempt to leave the situation to return to the comfort zone (Summala, 2007). Similar to comfort awareness, risk awareness is also deemed a vital component of driver decision making. Risk Homeostasis Theory (Wilde, 1976) suggests that drivers prefer to maintain a certain level of risk feeling. Fuller et al. also introduced Risk Allostasis Theory, which suggests that the preferred risk feeling of the driver indicates their actions (Fuller et al., 2008). The feeling of comfort and the feeling of risk provide different perspectives for studying traffic psychology. The Safety Margin Model was developed to model driver decision making process by taking the feeling of comfort as a primary variable (Summala, 2005). The Monitor Model considers the feeling of risk as the important factor for motivating drivers' actions (Vaa, 2007). However, the feeling of discomfort has rarely been discussed and analysed in previous studies, which is not completely equivalent to the feeling of risk.

Many researchers have attempted to reveal the relationship between driver awareness and encountered situations. Lewis-Evans et al. analysed the threshold effect of time headway referring to the risk, task difficulty, effort and comfort (Lewis-Evans et al., 2010). Also focusing on time headway, Siebert et al. identified the risk and comfort thresholds of car-following by collecting participants' self-reports on the perceived risky or uncomfortable distance (Siebert et al., 2017). However, using only time headway is insufficient for characterizing various driving situations.

Based on historical accident data, many studies have investigated the influencing factors that increase crash probability, which infers risk grading (Wang and Abdelaty, 2006;Abdel-Aty and Abdelwahab, 2004). However, they can barely cover the risk during normal driving. Thus, researchers have utilized safety measures for risk grading, including the time to collision (TTC) and deceleration rate to avoid the crash (DRAC), and analysed the rear-end crash risk and merging risk in multi-lanes based on vehicle trajectory data (Weng et al., 2018(Weng et al., , 2014;;Weng et al., 2015). In these studies, a general risk grading method is adopted for all drivers to discover general knowledge on traffic safety. However, facing the same situation, drivers' awareness grading could differ. Bärgman et al. quantified the comfort zone and dread zone boundaries in the context of left turning (Bärgman et al., 2015). They noted that each zone is determined by a dynamic function of the driver and the situation. Comparatively, the discomfort zone and varied discomfort grading patterns of drivers still remain unexplored. Overall, although many researchers have attempted to analyse comfort zones and safety margins, three major concerns have not been properly considered in existing studies. They are as follows:

(1) The feeling of discomfort lacks deep knowledge and comprehensive understanding and analysis. The feeling of risk is one part of the feeling of discomfort, which causes drivers to press the brake pedal. However, most previous studies ignored another part of the feeling of discomfort caused by the inefficient and inappropriate driving status, which causes drivers to press the gas pedal.

(2) The diversity of encountered situations have not been fully explored. Many works have focused on the threshold of temporal distance, i.e., time headway (Lewis-Evans et al., 2010;Siebert et al., 2017), while few have considered other kinetic variables, i.e., speed difference between the leading and following vehicles, for describing the dynamics of the encountered situations. (3) A proper quantitative analysis method is necessary to mine the potential patterns of drivers' discomfort grading. Although models for extracting driving patterns have been well established in the literature (Aljaafreh et al., 2012;Baer et al., 2011;Constantinescu et al., 2010;Qi et al., 2015), the studies are rarely managed from the perspective of recognizing the similarities and differences in drivers' situational discomfort.

The aim of this paper is to overcome these limitations and quantitatively mine and distinguish drivers' situational discomfort grading patterns. We define the feeling of discomfort as the sensation that triggers the driver's action to leave the current situation. This work could provide broader meaning to discomfort, and the actions on the brake pedal and gas pedal can be deemed as the intuitive reaction of drivers to the uncomfortable situations. For example, with relatively far/near distance from the front vehicle, one driver can perceive and attempt to remove the feeling of discomfort by leaving the situation using the gas/brake pedal (acceleration/deceleration). The study is performed using car-following data. Therefore, the speed difference between the leading and following (targeted) vehicle also contributes to build the dynamic situations. In the context of situational discomfort awareness, the latent Dirichlet allocation model is adopted to extract different potential patterns, thereby enhancing the understanding of driver heterogeneity.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: section II introduces the preliminaries of the study; section III details the methodology for quantitatively mining and distinguishing drivers' situational discomfort grading patterns; section IV shows and discusses the empirical results of car-following data; and section V gives the conclusion to the paper.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: The driving behaviour of 34 drivers is utilized in this study. These data were collected using an instrumented vehicle equipped with radar and a GPS device. The radar device, with a resolution of 0.1 (m), detects relative parameters such as the gap and speed difference between the leading vehicles and the instrumented vehicle. The GPS device, with a resolution of 0.1 (m/s), measures the speed and acceleration of the instrumented vehicle. Video cameras and a pre-processing approach help confirm the leading vehicle in car-following. The lane-changing behaviours and free flow conditions are excluded. The time gap (s), speed difference (m/s) and acceleration (m/s 2 ) during the car-following process are used in this research at a rate of 10 Hz. Each participating driver was asked to drive the instrumented vehicle along the same urban route. The route length is approximately 40 km and takes 2-3 h to complete. Before the formal experiment, the participants were also asked to conduct a test drive to become familiar with the instrumented vehicle. The participants had an average age of 38.0 years old (range: 23-58) and possessed a driver's licence for 11.8 years (range: 2-30). All drivers participated when they were sufficiently available and were compensated with an equal amount of money. These data were further pre-processed for studying the situational discomfort grading patterns.

paragraphs: The individual-specific effects in car-following affect the realism of the relevant models and hinder the practical applications (Brackstone and McDonald, 1999;Toledo, 2007). However, there are very few studies on capturing the differences among drivers from car-following data, especially in the context of situational discomfort awareness. Therefore, we collected car-following data of the drivers to examine their differences in this specific scenario. Note that the instructions, i.e., "please follow the front vehicle as closely as possible" or "please drive as quickly as possible", were not given to the drivers, so extra motives were not incorporated into driving and, the drivers could react (accelerate/decelerate) to the individually uncomfortable car-following situations within a certain safety margin.

paragraphs: In the driving process, the drivers encountered various situations and behaved differently. For simplicity, we divided the car-following situations encountered using the grid method. The temporal distance and speed difference are used to build the diverse situations. The gird size is × 1 1 (1 second of time gap and 1 metre/second of speed dif- ference). Through filtering and analysis, we obtained 467 encountered situations from the collected car-following data. The envelope of the car-following situations encountered is shown in Fig. 1. In the figure, each grid within the envelope represents a possible encountered situation during the urban car-following process. The centre value of the grid can be used to quantify the encountered situation. For example, the specified grid denotes the situation with a time gap of 2-3 seconds and a speed difference of 4-5 metres/second, which can be quantified by the grid centre vector as [2.5, 4.5].

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: Since real-world driving cannot guarantee consistent encountered situations for each driver, some drivers may lack the reaction data of some situations. Therefore, we attempted to complete the missing data using the k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) and the weighted average methods. This approach can also be considered k-NN regression. The k-NN method has been widely used in practice (Gallego et al., 2018;Samworth, 2012). The concept of the method is to classify the testing data based on the k-closest training data in the feature space. The reactions of the drivers were labelled using 3 classes, including a positive class (acceleration greater than 0.5 m/s 2 ), a moderate class (acceleration greater than -0.5 m/s 2 and less than 0.5 m/s 2 ) and a negative class (acceleration less than -0.5 m/s 2 ). In this paper, the training data include the driver's encountered time gap, the speed difference data, and the labels of their reactions. To calculate the distance between the missing situations and the collected data, the centre values of the grids shown in Fig. 1 are used to represent the possible situations. By applying the k-NN method (k=5 in this study), the k data with the smallest Euclidean distance to the missing situation are used to determine the label of the situation. The labelling approach reduces the influence of noise and errors which could significantly change the result of data completion. Then, the acceleration for each missing situation is calculated according to the weighted average method shown in equation (1). The acceleration values of neighbouring data points with the same label are considered and weighted by the inverse of their distance.

is the acceleration value of the j -th nearest neighbour data point with the same label as the missing data point i; and D ij represents the distance between the missing data point i and the training data point j.

Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: According to the description of Summala, the uncomfortable feeling of drivers could trigger them to leave the situation and return to the comfort zone (Summala, 2007). Therefore, in this research, we define the feeling of discomfort as the sensation that triggers the driver's action to leave the current situation. This definition gives broader meaning to the discomfort feeling that includes not only the feeling forcing drivers to press the brake pedal but also the feeling causing the driver to take actions on the gas pedal. In the discomfort feeling, the risk feeling is a special case that always attracts much attention since it is related to traffic safety. The risk feeling could occur when the drivers' behaviour exceeds a safe margin and triggers a hard press on the brake pedal. The discomfort feeling has wider range to describe all uncomfortable sensations. The relationship between comfort, discomfort and risk feeling can be depicted as in Fig. 2.

The actions to leave the current situation triggered by discomfort feeling can be quantified by the acceleration value. However, some acceleration values may not be completely attributed to the discomfort feeling. For example, due to unfamiliarity with the route, unfamiliarity with the vehicle or the influence of distracting factors (e.g., mobile phones), drivers may make errors, where the acceleration value fails to exactly reflect the situational discomfort feeling. Therefore, the participants of the experiment are required to become familiar with the route and vehicle in advance, and they are not allowed to talk with the experimenter or other people using a mobile phone. Additionally, no extra instructions are given to the participants, thus minimizing possible distractions. These measures can partly guarantee the collected reaction data of the drivers are mainly caused by the situational discomfort feeling; thus, the data are appropriate for quantifying the discomfort grading.

By implementing the aforementioned pre-processing and completion process, the encountered car following situations and the corresponding reaction for each driver and each situation can be achieved. The reactions of drivers, including the actions on the brake pedal and gas pedal, can intuitively describe the discomfort grading of the encountered situations. Larger absolute value of acceleration infers stronger willingness to leave the current situation and represents a higher discomfort grading. Therefore, we calculate the drivers' situational discomfort grading according to equation (2). This approach promises the identification of the similarities and differences of discomfort grading and provides a foundation for mining and distinguishing drivers' situational discomfort grading patterns.

where G i d represents the discomfort grading of driver d to situation i; In this first attempt to study situational discomfort grading, we simply use the absolute value of acceleration to represent the discomfort grading by setting = α 1. This is acceptable for quantifying the discomfort feeling with respect to the micro-situation (the physical situation in car-following). However, in the real driving process, other discomfort feelings may exist that are not triggered by the micro-situation. For example, in very congested traffic, the absolute value of acceleration is small, which only expresses the driver's discomfort feeling to the physical situation as in normal conditions. However, long periods of traffic congestion could add more discomfort feeling and may be expressed in other ways, e.g., pressing the horn. These additional discomfort feelings can change the original discomfort grading to the situation. Correspondingly, the adjustment parameter would vary. The influencing factors may include macro measures on traffic flow (e.g., average speed and occupancy rate) and weather conditions. With the help of psychological and physiological data, future research could focus on studying the relationship between these influencing factors and discomfort grading.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: The Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model is a typical topic model that has been widely applied in the field of data mining (Bando et al., 2013;Blei, 2012;Blei et al., 2003;Chen et al., 2017;Kinoshita et al., 2015;Qi et al., 2015). The main concept of the LDA model is to extract a small number of distributions to synthetically describe the overall data.

The graphical model of the standard LDA is shown in Fig. 3. In the text mining context, the word (w t ) in a document (d) is assumed to be determined by the distribution over topics (θ d ) and the latent topic distribution over the words (ϕ k ). For example, a word in a book is determined by the topics of the book, e.g., sport, and the most frequently used words for the topic, e.g., football. The multinomial distribution of topics and words with parameters θ and ϕ yield Dirichlet distributions with hyperparameters α and β, respectively. z t is the assigned topic of the word w t . In the model, the whole corpus (D), which includes large numbers of documents (d), could be represented by the distributions (θ) over several topics that follow the specific distributions (ϕ) over the words.

In previous research, Bando et al. used the LDA model to cluster driving scenes into drive topics (Bando et al., 2013). They deemed the driving scenes as "documents" and the physical features in each scene as "words". The drive topics represent the common distributions of the physical features in different scenes. Qi et al. proposed a modified LDA model and attempted to determine the commonness and individuality in different drivers' driving styles (Qi et 2015). They considered the driving behaviour tokens as "words" and extracted their potential driving states (topics) according to the repetition of the driving behaviour tokens (words) in different driving styles (documents).

In the context of discomfort awareness, we assume the discomfort grading of a driver is determined by the distribution patterns of the driver and each pattern's discomfort awareness distribution. Accordingly, we take the encountered car following situations as basic "words" w t and the drivers as "whole corpus" D. For different drivers, there are different pattern distributions θ. For different patterns, there are different discomfort awareness distributions ϕ. The unknown vari- ables θ and ϕ yield Dirichlet distributions with hyperparameters α and β, respectively. However, in the context of discomfort awareness, there is not a directly countable variable for using the LDA model. Therefore, situational discomfort grading is further translated into the number of unit discomfort occurrences, which allows for another interpretation to the same data. A discomfort grading value of 1 can be understood as 1 unit discomfort awareness. A discomfort grading of 80 by a driver to a situation represents 80 times of unit discomfort awareness are achieved when the driver meets the situation. In the translated data, each situation is encountered once by each driver. Therefore, the number of unit discomfort awareness for a situation equals the grading of the situation. Different drivers vary on the unit discomfort awareness times (discomfort grading) with respect to different situations. The amplification coefficient and Int function in equation ( 2) ensure the discomfort grading is a countable integer. Using the LDA model, the potential patterns (topics) and their distributions that explain the similarities and differences among drivers' situational discomfort grading can be quantitatively mined.

In the computation process of the LDA model, a Markov Chain of the potential patterns (topics) of the situations (words) can be developed by initially assigning a random pattern to each situation and iteratively assigning the patterns to the situations according to the current condition. The potential pattern of each situation (z t ) can be selected in each iteration according to the probability calculated in equation ( 3). The derivation process is omitted in this paper due to the length limit and interested readers can refer to the relevant research (Blei et al., 2003;Qi et al., 2016). The unknown drivers' distributions over the potential patterns (θ) and potential patterns' discomfort awareness distributions over situations (ϕ) can be determined when the Markov Chain reaches the stationary distribution. with pattern k for driver d and the amount of the unit discomfort awareness occurring for situation t with pattern k for all drivers D; W is the total discomfort occurring in the situations; - Z d t ( , ) is all the patterns of discomfort occurring in situations in the current condition except for the discomfort occurring in situation t for driver d ; and -i does not include the discomfort occuring situation d t ( , ) in the calculation. The Gibbs Sampler algorithm, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, is implemented. At the convergence of the Gibbs Sampling process, the drivers' distributions over potential patterns (θ) and the potential patterns' discomfort awareness distributions over encountered car following situations (ϕ) can be determined. The former explains the similarities and differences among drivers in the context of discomfort awareness and the latter illustrates the meaning of each potential pattern. In this way, the high-dimensional situational discomfort grading problem is reduced to a low-dimensional potential pattern distribution. Each potential pattern represents a different discomfort awareness pattern, which can be observed by depicting ϕ as shown in Fig. 5 in the next section. Different combinations of potential patterns, as shown in Fig. 4(a) in the next section, can be used to form the drivers' unique discomfort grading mode. Similar or different distributions over potential patterns (θ) can synthetically manifest the similarities and differences among the drivers' situational discomfort grading mode. The achieved ϕ and θ help improve the understanding of driver discomfort awareness and provide a new perspective for distinguishing and classifying drivers.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: As alluded to earlier, we collected the car-following data of 34 drivers and selected 467 encountered car following situations that consider both the temporal distance and speed difference. According to the reaction data of each driver to each situation, the drivers' situational discomfort grading can be calculated according to equation (2), in which the amplification coefficient is manually set as 100 in this study. By doing so, the discomfort grading of a driver can be expressed by a vector with 467 dimensions.

By treating different drivers as "documents" and situations as "words", the LDA model is used to quantitatively mine the situational discomfort grading patterns. Five potential patterns are extracted and the drivers' distributions over these potential patterns (θ) are shown in Fig. 4(a). For example, driver 25# possesses a higher probability on pattern 4 and a lower probability on both pattern 2 and pattern 5. The individual discomfort awareness is shown to vary between drivers. More importantly, with the help of the LDA model, a driver's characteristics on discomfort awareness can be represented by a vector with 5 dimensions, which is more conducive for understanding and analysing the individual-specific effects. The results can be used to distinguish and classify drivers. As shown in Fig. 4(b), three driver clusters are obtained by implementing the well-known Fuzzy C-Mean (FCM) clustering method (Davies and Bouldin, 1979;Dunn, 1973) on the achieved drivers' distributions data.

As another output of the LDA model, the potential patterns' discomfort awareness distributions over the encountered car-following situations (ϕ) reveal the meaning of the extracted patterns, as shown in Fig. 5. For each pattern, the time gap and speed difference are taken as the horizontal axis and vertical axis, and the discomfort awareness distribution over each encountered situation is plotted using different colours in the figure. For instance, for pattern 1 (Fig. 5(a)), the situations with a larger time gap and larger positive speed difference (the speed of the leading vehicle is greater than the speed of the following vehicle) exhibit higher probability, which reflect the drivers' discomfort awareness to the situation of a large gap and receding leading vehicle, while for pattern 2 (Fig. 5(b)), the situations with a smaller time gap and larger negative speed difference (the speed of the leading vehicle is less than the speed of the following vehicle) display higher probability, which manifests the drivers' higher level of discomfort awareness to the situation of a small gap and the approaching leading vehicle. Similarly, for patterns 3, 4 and 5, the discomfort awareness is situationally varied.

With more driving behaviour data of different drivers, the ϕ values can be more stable and the θ values become more comprehensive. Given the car following data of a driver, the driver's distribution over different patterns (θ d ) can be determined using the Gibbs Sampler algorithm with a fixed ϕ. This approach may be helpful for identifying and distinguishing the driver. Furthermore, as a generative model, the LDA model is also able to create a new driver's situational discomfort grading for relevant modelling or simulation by mixing the extracted potential patterns with a certain proportion generated by the learned Dirichlet distribution.

As alluded to earlier, we collected the car-following data of 34 drivers and selected 467 encountered car following situations that consider both the temporal distance and speed difference. According to the reaction data of each driver to each situation, the drivers' situational discomfort grading can be calculated according to equation (2), in which the amplification coefficient is manually set as 100 in this study. By doing so, the discomfort grading of a driver can be expressed by a vector with 467 dimensions.

By treating different drivers as "documents" and situations as "words", the LDA model is used to quantitatively mine the situational discomfort grading patterns. Five potential patterns are extracted and the drivers' distributions over these potential patterns (θ) are shown in Fig. 4(a). For example, driver 25# possesses a higher probability on pattern 4 and a lower probability on both pattern 2 and pattern 5. The individual discomfort awareness is shown to vary between drivers. More importantly, with the help of the LDA model, a driver's characteristics on discomfort awareness can be represented by a vector with 5 dimensions, which is more conducive for understanding and analysing the individual-specific effects. The results can be used to distinguish and classify drivers. As shown in Fig. 4(b), three driver clusters are obtained by implementing the well-known Fuzzy C-Mean (FCM) clustering method (Davies and Bouldin, 1979;Dunn, 1973) on the achieved drivers' distributions data.

As another output of the LDA model, the potential patterns' discomfort awareness distributions over the encountered car-following situations (ϕ) reveal the meaning of the extracted patterns, as shown in Fig. 5. For each pattern, the time gap and speed difference are taken as the horizontal axis and vertical axis, and the discomfort awareness distribution over each encountered situation is plotted using different colours in the figure. For instance, for pattern 1 (Fig. 5(a)), the situations with a larger time gap and larger positive speed difference (the speed of the leading vehicle is greater than the speed of the following vehicle) exhibit higher probability, which reflect the drivers' discomfort awareness to the situation of a large gap and receding leading vehicle, while for pattern 2 (Fig. 5(b)), the situations with a smaller time gap and larger negative speed difference (the speed of the leading vehicle is less than the speed of the following vehicle) display higher probability, which manifests the drivers' higher level of discomfort awareness to the situation of a small gap and the approaching leading vehicle. Similarly, for patterns 3, 4 and 5, the discomfort awareness is situationally varied.

With more driving behaviour data of different drivers, the ϕ values can be more stable and the θ values become more comprehensive. Given the car following data of a driver, the driver's distribution over different patterns (θ d ) can be determined using the Gibbs Sampler algorithm with a fixed ϕ. This approach may be helpful for identifying and distinguishing the driver. Furthermore, as a generative model, the LDA model is also able to create a new driver's situational discomfort grading for relevant modelling or simulation by mixing the extracted potential patterns with a certain proportion generated by the learned Dirichlet distribution.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: Compared with previous studies, the meaning of discomfort is extended in this paper by considering all the feelings that cause a driver to leave the current situation. The reaction of a driver can intuitively reflect their discomfort grading to the encountered situation depicted by the time gap and speed difference. Although the boundaries, thresholds and zones of comfort or risk have been well studied (Bärgman et al., 2015;Lewis-Evans et al., 2010;Siebert et al., 2017), the drivers' potential patterns in situational discomfort grading has rarely been discussed. This study fills gaps in previous studies by using a new discomfort grading method and the latent Dirichlet allocation model.

Technically, every driver shapes their own unique discomfort grading mode, just as the different distributions over the extracted patterns suggest in Fig. 4(a). However, in practical applications, some basic or general options must be provided to drivers when more individual data is unavailable. Therefore, as the general options, the clustering centres data (the proportion of the extracted patterns of the clustering centres) in Fig. 4(b) can be used to construct the situational discomfort grading of driver clusters that can be deemed typical drivers in the current samples. The discomfort grading of three clusters are shown in Fig. 6(a), Fig. 6(c) and Fig. 6(e). For each cluster, the time gap and speed difference are taken as the horizontal axis and vertical axis, and the discomfort grading over each encountered situation is depicted using different colours in the figure. Red/Blue represent higher/lower discomfort grading. The discomfort awareness for the three clusters is shown to situationally vary. For example, compared with driver cluster 2, driver cluster 1 behaves more cautiously, as it presents weaker discomfort awareness for the situation of a moderate gap and receding leading vehicle and stronger discomfort awareness for the situation of a larger gap and approaching leading vehicle, while driver cluster 3 behaves more aggressively, since it has weaker discomfort awareness for the situation of a larger gap and approaching leading vehicle and stronger discomfort awareness for the situation of a larger gap and receding leading vehicle. If a threshold of grading is set between the comfort and discomfort, the comfort and discomfort zone of these typical drivers can be described as Fig. 6(b), Fig. 6(d) and Fig. 6(f). The threshold is specified as 22 in this paper, which can be calculated according to equation (2) with equal small acceleration values ± 0.5. A discomfort grading value greater or less than the threshold is reset to 1 (discomfort) or 0 (comfort). Then, also using surface plotting, the reset values over the encountered situations are depicted. From the figures, different driver clusters have varied comfort/discomfort zones. For example, the more cautious driver cluster 1 has a larger comfort zone with positive speed difference (drivers are relatively insensitive to the leading vehicle acceleration), while the more aggressive driver cluster 3 has a larger comfort zone with negative speed difference (drivers are partially insensitive to the leading vehicle deceleration). Furthermore, a common comfort zone can also be discovered by fusing the comfort zone of the three clusters. As shown in Fig. 7, besides the zone with a small speed difference, a fitting line can be used to express the shape of the common comfort zone, from which the basic comfortable situation can be observed and calculated. The acceptable positive speed differences mainly appear within a time gap of 6 s and decreases along with decreasing time gap, while the acceptable negative speed differences mainly occur between 4-12 seconds and tend to be larger with increasing time gap.

Understanding the similarities and differences in discomfort awareness among drivers is vital for developing better driving behaviour models and more feasible ADASs. For example, taking the feeling of discomfort as a primary variable, a more comprehensive model can be built to understand driver decision making and predict driving behaviour. Furthermore, by considering the discomfort zone and discomfort grading patterns of drivers, the customized ADAS could achieve better performance and bring satisfactory individual experience. Especially in the initial usage phase of ADAS, allowing drivers to feel discomfort many times could reduce the trust of the systems and influence further use. The research results also provide a new perspective for distinguishing and classifying drivers according to their situational discomfort awareness, which can be used to establish Usage-Based Insurance (UBI) and adopting differentiated policies.

There is some room for improvement in this study. For example, we defined the discomfort grading based on the driver's absolute value of acceleration. This may provide more instant and intuitive answers of drivers encountering dynamic situations that change too quickly to apply the questionnaires (Ishibashi et al., 2007;Reason et al., 1990). However, this definition fails to differentiate the impact of acceleration and deceleration on the feeling of discomfort. With the same absolute value, deceleration may express a higher level of discomfort than acceleration, as the behaviours on the brake pedal are more related with traffic safety. In this basic and first attempt to study discomfort grading, we only grade the discomfort level from the drivers' physical reactions. Incorporating the physiological information of drivers, such as heart rate variability (HRV), galvanic skin response (GSR) and electroencephalograph (EEG) (Wang et al., 2017;Singh et al., 2014;Yang et al., 2018), could properly settle this imbalance problem between acceleration and deceleration by describing the drivers' most underlying feeling. Therefore, more studies on this aspect, especially analysing situational discomfort grading based on physiological information, are necessary.

Certainly, we agree that the discomfort grading pattern of a driver is not consistent. Driving experience and skill improvement may change the driver's discomfort grading to the encountered situations. The change of drivers' discomfort grading patterns with increasing driving experience/skill is also a challenge for future work, since it indicates the drivers' learning process and mechanism.

sections.paper_id: Qi and Guan - 2019 - Quantitatively mining and distinguishing situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car
paragraphs: In this paper, we aimed to quantitatively mine and distinguish the situational discomfort grading patterns of drivers from car following data. The feeling of discomfort was defined as the sensation that triggers a driver's action to leave the current situation and the discomfort grading is intuitively calculated based on the drivers' situational reaction data. Using an LDA model, five potential patterns and their distributions were extracted to describe the drivers' similarities and differences in discomfort awareness. Moreover, the results of the LDA model can be further analysed to classify drivers and identify the typical comfort and discomfort zones of drivers. Future studies should incorporate the physiological information of the drivers and analyse the individual changes in situational discomfort grading.
